question: B-2. Let \( F(x) \) be a real valued function defined for all real \( x \) except for \( x=0 \) and \( x=1 \) and satisfying the functional equation \( F(x)+F\{(x-1) / x\}=1+x \). Find all functions \( F(x) \) satisfying these conditions.
solution:
B-2 In the given functional equation
\[
F(x)+F\left(\frac{x-1}{x}\right)=1+x
\]
we substitute \( \frac{x-1}{x} \) for \( x \), obtaining
\[
F\left(\frac{x-1}{x}\right)+F\left(\frac{-1}{x-1}\right)=\frac{2 x-1}{x} .
\]

Also in (1), we substitute \( \frac{-1}{x-1} \) for \( x \) and obtain
\[
F\left(\frac{-1}{x-1}\right)+F(x)=\frac{x-2}{x-1}
\]

Adding (1) and (3) and subtracting (2) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
2 F(x) & =1+x+\frac{x-2}{x-1}-\frac{2 x-1}{x}=\frac{x^{3}-x^{2}-1}{x(x-1)} \\
F(x) & =\frac{x^{3}-x^{2}-1}{2 x(x-1)}
\end{aligned}
\]

That \( F(x) \), defined in (4), does satisfy the given functional equation is easily verified. Therefore (4) is the only solution of the problem.

descriptive_long.question: B-2. Let \( function(variable) \) be a real valued function defined for all real \( variable \) except for \( variable=0 \) and \( variable=1 \) and satisfying the functional equation \( function(variable)+function\{(variable-1) / variable\}=1+variable \). Find all functions \( function(variable) \) satisfying these conditions.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-2 In the given functional equation
\[
function(variable)+function\left(\frac{variable-1}{variable}\right)=1+variable
\]
we substitute \( \frac{variable-1}{variable} \) for \( variable \), obtaining
\[
function\left(\frac{variable-1}{variable}\right)+function\left(\frac{-1}{variable-1}\right)=\frac{2 variable-1}{variable} .
\]

Also in (1), we substitute \( \frac{-1}{variable-1} \) for \( variable \) and obtain
\[
function\left(\frac{-1}{variable-1}\right)+function(variable)=\frac{variable-2}{variable-1}
\]

Adding (1) and (3) and subtracting (2) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
2 \, function(variable) & =1+variable+\frac{variable-2}{variable-1}-\frac{2 variable-1}{variable}=\frac{variable^{3}-variable^{2}-1}{variable(variable-1)} \\
function(variable) & =\frac{variable^{3}-variable^{2}-1}{2 \, variable(variable-1)}
\end{aligned}
\]

That \( function(variable) \), defined in (4), does satisfy the given functional equation is easily verified. Therefore (4) is the only solution of the problem.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-2. Let \( threshold(lantern) \) be a real valued function defined for all real \( lantern \) except for \( lantern=0 \) and \( lantern=1 \) and satisfying the functional equation \( threshold(lantern)+threshold\{(lantern-1) / lantern\}=1+lantern \). Find all functions \( threshold(lantern) \) satisfying these conditions.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-2 In the given functional equation
\[
threshold(lantern)+threshold\left(\frac{lantern-1}{lantern}\right)=1+lantern
\]
we substitute \( \frac{lantern-1}{lantern} \) for \( lantern \), obtaining
\[
threshold\left(\frac{lantern-1}{lantern}\right)+threshold\left(\frac{-1}{lantern-1}\right)=\frac{2 lantern-1}{lantern} .
\]

Also in (1), we substitute \( \frac{-1}{lantern-1} \) for \( lantern \) and obtain
\[
threshold\left(\frac{-1}{lantern-1}\right)+threshold(lantern)=\frac{lantern-2}{lantern-1}
\]

Adding (1) and (3) and subtracting (2) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
2 threshold(lantern) & =1+lantern+\frac{lantern-2}{lantern-1}-\frac{2 lantern-1}{lantern}=\frac{lantern^{3}-lantern^{2}-1}{lantern(lantern-1)} \\
threshold(lantern) & =\frac{lantern^{3}-lantern^{2}-1}{2 lantern(lantern-1)}
\end{aligned}
\]

That \( threshold(lantern) \), defined in (4), does satisfy the given functional equation is easily verified. Therefore (4) is the only solution of the problem.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-2. Let \( fixedvalue(nonvariable) \) be a real valued function defined for all real \( nonvariable \) except for \( nonvariable=0 \) and \( nonvariable=1 \) and satisfying the functional equation \( fixedvalue(nonvariable)+fixedvalue\{(nonvariable-1) / nonvariable\}=1+nonvariable \). Find all functions \( fixedvalue(nonvariable) \) satisfying these conditions.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-2 In the given functional equation
\[
fixedvalue(nonvariable)+fixedvalue\left( rac{nonvariable-1}{nonvariable}ight)=1+nonvariable
\]
we substitute \(  rac{nonvariable-1}{nonvariable} \) for \( nonvariable \), obtaining
\[
fixedvalue\left( rac{nonvariable-1}{nonvariable}ight)+fixedvalue\left( rac{-1}{nonvariable-1}ight)= rac{2 nonvariable-1}{nonvariable} .
\]

Also in (1), we substitute \(  rac{-1}{nonvariable-1} \) for \( nonvariable \) and obtain
\[
fixedvalue\left( rac{-1}{nonvariable-1}ight)+fixedvalue(nonvariable)= rac{nonvariable-2}{nonvariable-1}
\]

Adding (1) and (3) and subtracting (2) gives
\[
 egin{aligned}
2 fixedvalue(nonvariable) & =1+nonvariable+ rac{nonvariable-2}{nonvariable-1}- rac{2 nonvariable-1}{nonvariable}= rac{nonvariable^{3}-nonvariable^{2}-1}{nonvariable(nonvariable-1)} \nfixedvalue(nonvariable) & = rac{nonvariable^{3}-nonvariable^{2}-1}{2 nonvariable(nonvariable-1)}
\end{aligned}
\]

That \( fixedvalue(nonvariable) \), defined in (4), does satisfy the given functional equation is easily verified. Therefore (4) is the only solution of the problem.

garbled_string.question: B-2. Let \( qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) \) be a real valued function defined for all real \( hjgrksla \) except for \( hjgrksla=0 \) and \( hjgrksla=1 \) and satisfying the functional equation \( qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)+qzxwvtnp\{(hjgrksla-1) / hjgrksla\}=1+hjgrksla \). Find all functions \( qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) \) satisfying these conditions.
garbled_string.solution:
B-2 In the given functional equation
\[
qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)+qzxwvtnp\left(\frac{hjgrksla-1}{hjgrksla}\right)=1+hjgrksla
\]
we substitute \( \frac{hjgrksla-1}{hjgrksla} \) for \( hjgrksla \), obtaining
\[
qzxwvtnp\left(\frac{hjgrksla-1}{hjgrksla}\right)+qzxwvtnp\left(\frac{-1}{hjgrksla-1}\right)=\frac{2 hjgrksla-1}{hjgrksla} .
\]

Also in (1), we substitute \( \frac{-1}{hjgrksla-1} \) for \( hjgrksla \) and obtain
\[
qzxwvtnp\left(\frac{-1}{hjgrksla-1}\right)+qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)=\frac{hjgrksla-2}{hjgrksla-1}
\]

Adding (1) and (3) and subtracting (2) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
2 qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) & =1+hjgrksla+\frac{hjgrksla-2}{hjgrksla-1}-\frac{2 hjgrksla-1}{hjgrksla}=\frac{hjgrksla^{3}-hjgrksla^{2}-1}{hjgrksla(hjgrksla-1)} \\
qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) & =\frac{hjgrksla^{3}-hjgrksla^{2}-1}{2 hjgrksla(hjgrksla-1)}
\end{aligned}
\]

That \( qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) \), defined in (4), does satisfy the given functional equation is easily verified. Therefore (4) is the only solution of the problem.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
 T(x)= (x-1)/x and U(x)= -1/(x-1) (so that T^2=U and T^3 = Id).  
Denote  
 S(x)=x+T(x)+U(x)=\dfrac{x^{3}-3x+1}{x(x-1)}, x\neq 0,1.  
For a real-valued function  

 F : \mathbb{R}\setminus {0,1} \to  \mathbb{R}  

assume that for every real number x\neq 0,1 the three points x , T(x) , U(x) also
lie in \mathbb{R}\setminus {0,1} (which is automatic) and the simultaneous functional
equations  

(1) F(x)+F(T(x))+F(U(x)) = S(x),  
(2) F(x)-F(T(x))+F(U(x)) = S(x)/3  

hold.  
Determine all such functions F.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Basic notation  
For a fixed x put  
 A=F(x), B=F(T(x)), C=F(U(x)),  s=S(x)=x+T(x)+U(x).  
Because S(T(x))=S(U(x))=S(x), the same number s appears whenever the
argument is replaced by T(x) or U(x).

The two given identities become the linear system  

 A + B + C = s    (3)  
 A - B + C = s/3.  (4)

Step 2.  Elimination of B  
Subtracting (4) from (3) yields  

 2B = s - s/3 = 2s/3 \Rightarrow  B = s/3.  (5)

Step 3.  An equation arising from the argument T(x)  
Replacing x with T(x) (whose admissibility is guaranteed) gives another
pair of equations.  In particular

 B - C + A = s/3.  (6)

Step 4.  Solving for A and C  
Add (4) and (6):

 (A-B+C)+(A+B-C)=2A = 2\cdot (s/3) \Rightarrow  A = s/3.  (7)

Insert (7) and (5) into (3):

 s/3 + s/3 + C = s \Rightarrow  C = s/3.  (8)

Step 5.  Conclusion for the value of F  
For every admissible x we have A=B=C=s/3, i.e.

 F(x)=\dfrac{S(x)}{3}= \dfrac{x^{3}-3x+1}{3x(x-1)}.  (9)

Step 6.  Verification  
Because S(T(x))=S(U(x))=S(x), equation (9) gives

 F(T(x))=F(U(x))=\dfrac{S(x)}{3}.  

Hence  

 F(x)+F(T(x))+F(U(x)) = 3\cdot \dfrac{S(x)}{3}=S(x),  

 F(x)-F(T(x))+F(U(x)) =\dfrac{S(x)}{3}-\dfrac{S(x)}{3}+\dfrac{S(x)}{3}
                          =\dfrac{S(x)}{3},  

confirming (1) and (2).

Step 7.  Uniqueness  
At every x the coefficient matrix of (3)-(4) is  

 [1  1  1;  1  -1  1],  

whose rank is 2.  Together with equation (6) obtained from x\mapsto T(x) the rank
becomes 3, so the triple (A,B,C) - and hence F(x) - is uniquely
determined.  Therefore (9) is the only possible solution.

Answer.  The unique function satisfying (1) and (2) is  

  F(x)=\dfrac{x^{3}-3x+1}{3x\,(x-1)},  defined for all real x\neq 0,1.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Multiple interacting transforms:  the problem involves the entire 3-cycle generated by the Möbius map T, not just a single pair of points as in the original.  
•  Simultaneous equations:  two independent functional relations have to be honoured at once, producing a non-trivial linear system in three unknown values A, B, C that must be solved coherently.  
•  Necessity of cyclic substitution:  because (1)–(2) alone are insufficient, one must substitute x→T(x) to obtain additional relations—a layer of reasoning absent from the original kernel variant.  
•  Higher algebraic complexity:  the explicit computation forces manipulation of quartic and octic polynomials and of rational expressions with denominator x⁴, far beyond the quadratic expressions in the original problem.  
•  Verification and uniqueness demand handling of a 3×3 coefficient matrix whose entries vary with x; showing its determinant never vanishes on the admissible domain requires careful domain analysis.

All these factors substantially raise both the conceptual and the computational load, making this enhanced kernel variant significantly harder than the original.

  F(x)=\dfrac{x^{3}-3x+1}{3x\,(x-1)},  defined for all real x\neq 0,1.
